description: Light Emitting Diode — emits light in forward bias. Requires current limiting resistor.

absolute_max.reverse_voltage: 5V typical (LEDs have very low reverse breakdown)

pins.name: A
pins.description: Anode — longer lead on THT, connected to positive supply through current limiting resistor
pins.requirements: MUST have current limiting resistor in series. R = (Vsupply - Vf) / If. Example: 5V supply, red LED Vf=2V, If=10mA → R = (5-2)/0.01 = 300Ω

pins.name: K
pins.description: Cathode — shorter lead on THT, flat side on lens, connected to GND

required_passives.reason: Current limiting resistor is MANDATORY. Without it the LED draws unlimited current and immediately fails.

application_circuits.name: GPIO indicator LED
application_circuits.description: GPIO → R (330Ω for 3.3V, 470Ω for 5V) → LED anode → LED cathode → GND

common_mistakes: Connecting LED without current limiting resistor destroys it instantly
Installing backwards — anode (long lead) to positive, cathode (short lead/flat side) to GND
GPIO pin max current is typically 20–25mA — keep LED current under this limit
Forward voltage varies by color: Red ~2V, Green ~2.1V, Blue/White ~3.2V — use correct Vf in resistor calculation
Reverse voltage rating is very low (~5V) — do not apply reverse voltage
notes: Typical forward voltages: Red=2.0V, Yellow=2.1V, Green=2.1V, Blue=3.2V, White=3.2V at 20mA. Typical operating current 10–20mA for indicators. For high-brightness indication, 5mA is often sufficient. R formula: R = (Vsupply - Vf) / If_desired.
